candidate_problems.text: How can 'developmental dependence' of the interpreter system be operationalized and measured in a way that distinguishes capture from legitimate epistemic reliance on regulated actors? The conjecture explains a mechanism, but it leaves open the boundary problem: agencies must depend on outside expertise to function, so what observable structures, feedback loops, or thresholds mark the transition from necessary coordination to systematic canalization by industry?

What institutional designs can reduce layered regulatory dependence without destroying competence, speed, and administrability—the very problems the existing arrangements originally solved? The conjecture proposes leverage points such as independent knowledge generation and adversarial review, but the unresolved tension is whether these reforms can remain load-bearing under real constraints or whether they simply reintroduce dependence, delay, or new forms of capture elsewhere in the system.

Is the conjecture's causal claim actually stronger than competing explanations such as corruption, ideology, principal-agent drift, or state capacity failure, and under what conditions? The open question is whether layered interpretive dependence is a general theory of recurring capture or only one mechanism among several. Exploring when this mechanism dominates, interacts with, or is overridden by other causes would test the conjecture's explanatory reach and constraint.
